message:
chore(beads): sync issue tracker with latest closed/updated statuses

Update .beads/issues.jsonl to reflect recent issue lifecycle changes:
- Closed issues with resolution notes from agent sessions
  (resize artifact root-cause analysis, fault model documentation,
  data structure implementation completions)
- Updated timestamps and close_reason fields on resolved beads
- Reflects work completed by multiple agents (LavenderCastle,
  PinkDune, and others) across recent sessions

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation is complete for the stated scope and behavior is validated.
- Unit tests are added/updated for core logic and edge cases.
- Integration and/or e2e coverage is updated where this task affects multi-component behavior.
- Structured telemetry/logging is added for observability and regression triage.

Identified 5 fault classes (F1: PTY-terminal dimension gap, F2: non-atomic render reads, F3: tab fanout inconsistency, F4: stale intent commit, F5: cursor position transient). Produced concrete fault model in docs/resize-artifact-fault-model-wa-1u90p.4.1.md with artifact class mapping, severity assessment, mitigation design (M1-M4), and recommended execution order. Primary cause of stretched text is F1 (PTY resized before terminal, creating window where child output uses new dimensions but terminal has old).  {"id":"ft-1u90p.4.10","title":"[Subtask wa-1u90p.4.2] Implement rollback/fallback handling for failed frame commits","description":"## Scope
Add robust rollback path when a candidate frame fails validation/commit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Failure-injection tests verify clean fallback to last-good frame.
- No user-visible artifact leaks during rollback scenarios.
- Telemetry captures rollback cause taxonomy.","notes":"Implemented rollback/fallback handling for failed resize frame commits in frankenterm/term/src/screen.rs. Added rollback cause taxonomy via LastGoodFrameRollbackCause (resize_commit_validation, forced_failure_injection) and structured telemetry logs: last_good_frame_rollback cause=<...> action=<applied|missing_snapshot>. Added rollback lifecycle counters (rollback_count, rollback_missing_snapshot_count) and snapshot carry-over of dpi for complete restoration. Wired resize commit failure path to rollback to retained last-good frame and added deterministic failure injection for tests. New tests: last_good_frame_rolls_back_after_forced_resize_commit_failure, last_good_frame_rollback_tracks_missing_snapshot_failures.  {"id":"ft-283h4.3","title":"Formal mux protocol verification via TLA+","description":"# Formal Mux Protocol Verification via TLA+

## Why This Is Novel
No terminal emulator in existence has formal protocol verification. This is the purest expression of alien-artifact-coding. TLA+ finds bugs that no amount of testing can find — Lamport proved this mathematically. Amazon uses TLA+ for S3, DynamoDB, EBS, and found critical bugs in all of them.

## What We Model

### Spec 1: PDU Exchange Protocol
States: Idle, Sending, Receiving, Error, Reconnecting
Properties to verify:
- **Safety**: Messages are never lost (every sent PDU is eventually received or error-reported)
- **Ordering**: PDUs are delivered in sequence order
- **No duplicates**: Each PDU is processed exactly once
- **Liveness**: Every operation eventually completes (no indefinite blocking)

### Spec 2: Snapshot/Restore Lifecycle
States: Normal, Capturing, Writing, Stored, Restoring, Restored
Properties to verify:
- **Consistency**: Snapshot captures a consistent point-in-time state
- **Atomicity**: Restore is all-or-nothing (no partial state)
- **No data loss**: snapshot(state) followed by restore() reproduces state exactly
- **Idempotency**: restore(restore(snapshot)) == restore(snapshot)

### Spec 3: Concurrent Pane Management
Multiple panes being created, destroyed, resized, focused simultaneously.
Properties to verify:
- **No orphans**: Every created pane is eventually destroyed or persisted
- **No leaks**: Resources (FDs, memory) are freed when panes close
- **No deadlocks**: No circular wait conditions under any interleaving
- **Liveness**: Every create/destroy operation completes in bounded steps

### Spec 4: WAL Correctness (if #2 is implemented)
Properties:
- **Durability**: Mutations written to WAL survive crashes
- **Replay equivalence**: Replaying WAL produces identical state to original
- **Compaction safety**: Compaction preserves all information

## Deliverables
- `docs/formal/mux_protocol.tla` — PDU exchange spec
- `docs/formal/snapshot_lifecycle.tla` — Snapshot spec
- `docs/formal/concurrent_panes.tla` — Concurrency spec
- `docs/formal/wal_correctness.tla` — WAL spec (if applicable)
- `docs/formal/VERIFICATION_RESULTS.md` — Properties verified, model checking results, any bugs found
- The TLA+ specs serve as the AUTHORITATIVE protocol documentation

## State Space
The mux protocol has ~15-20 states, 5-8 actions per state. Model checking with TLC is tractable (millions of states in minutes). We're not verifying a distributed system — just a client-server protocol with finite state.

## Tooling
- TLA+ Toolbox or `tlc` CLI for model checking
- PlusCal (algorithmic language) for easier spec writing
- Apalache (symbolic model checker) for infinite-state properties

## Testing
- Model checking all specs with TLC — must pass with zero violations
- Any bugs found are documented and fixed in the Rust implementation
- Specs updated when protocol changes

## Cost/Benefit
- Cost: ~2-3 days for a developer familiar with TLA+
- Benefit: Mathematical certainty about protocol correctness, forever
- ROI: One prevented data-loss bug pays for the entire effort

## Depends on
- wa-2umk2 (FrankenTerm in-tree) — need to understand the protocol to model it
- Does NOT depend on implementation work — verification is orthogonal

---

## Cross-References
- **wa-2dd4s.5** (FrankenMux wire protocol): The TLA+ specs in this bead formally model the wire protocol defined in wa-2dd4s.5. Any protocol changes in wa-2dd4s.5 must be reflected here, and any bugs found by TLC model checking should feed back as protocol fixes.
- **wa-283h4.13** (Self-stabilizing mux protocol): The self-stabilization convergence invariants should be modeled as TLA+ temporal properties here. Spec 1 (PDU exchange) should include self-stabilization reconciliation messages as additional actions.

## TLC Model Checker Configuration
Run TLC with explicit state space bounds to keep model checking tractable:
- Max states: 10M (sufficient for protocol with ~20 states × 8 actions)
- Max depth: 50 transitions (bounds liveness checking)
- Symmetry reduction: apply symmetry sets for pane IDs to reduce state space by N!
- Liveness checking: use `-lncheck` flag for temporal property verification
- CI integration: `tlc -workers auto -deadlock mux_protocol.tla` in GitHub Actions

## Runtime Property Validation via Proptest
While TLA+/TLC verifies the model, proptest validates that the Rust implementation matches the model:

```
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn pdu_ordering_preserved(pdus in prop::collection::vec(arb_pdu(), 1..100)) {
        // Send PDUs through codec pipeline, verify arrival order matches send order
    }

    #[test]
    fn snapshot_restore_roundtrip(state in arb_mux_state()) {
        // snapshot(state) -> restore() -> assert_eq!(original, restored)
    }

    #[test]
    fn no_duplicate_delivery(pdus in prop::collection::vec(arb_pdu(), 1..50),
                             drop_indices in prop::collection::vec(0..50usize, 0..10)) {
        // Simulate drops and retransmits, verify each PDU delivered exactly once
    }

    #[test]
    fn convergence_from_arbitrary_state(
        initial in arb_corrupted_mux_state(),
        reconciliation_rounds in 1..5u32
    ) {
        // Verify protocol converges to legitimate state within bounded rounds
    }
}
```","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ScarletBarn","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:06:41.536788Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:40:33.381750Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-283h4.3","depends_on_id":"ft-283h4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-283h4.3","depends_on_id":"ft-2umk2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":190,"issue_id":"ft-283h4.3","author":"ScarletBarn","text":"Implemented formal verification artifacts under docs/formal: mux_protocol.tla (updated with bounded MaxMsgId + fairness Spec), snapshot_lifecycle.tla, concurrent_panes.tla, wal_correctness.tla, with companion TLC configs and docs/formal/VERIFICATION_RESULTS.md.  {"id":"ft-283h4.4","title":"io_uring zero-copy mux I/O pipeline","description":"# io_uring Zero-Copy Mux I/O Pipeline

## Skills: /alien-artifact-coding (formal correctness), /extreme-software-optimization (profile-driven)

## Why This Is Novel
No existing bead covers the kernel I/O syscall layer. wa-3kxe.4 covers userspace data flow. wa-1lc2 covers protocol-level batching. wa-brc7d covers the async runtime. This covers the SYSTEM CALL layer — the boundary between userspace and kernel.

## The Problem
50 panes doing ~1100 syscalls/sec, each costing 1-5μs context switch = ~1-5ms/sec just in syscall transitions.

## io_uring Solution (Linux 5.6+)
1. **Submission batching**: 100 I/O ops in a single syscall
2. **Completion batching**: Reap 100 completions in single syscall
3. **Registered buffers**: Pre-register, kernel uses directly (zero-copy)
4. **Kernel polling**: SQPOLL mode eliminates submission syscalls
5. **Fixed files**: Pre-register FDs, eliminate fd-lookup overhead

## Platform Support — CRITICAL
- **Linux 5.6+**: Full io_uring support (primary target)
- **Linux <5.6**: Fall back to epoll
- **macOS**: Fall back to kqueue — no io_uring equivalent, but kqueue has some batching via kevent() multi-event submission
- **Feature flag**: `io-uring` enables the Linux fast path
- This bead is primarily a Linux optimization. macOS uses kqueue fallback which is already decent.

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct IoUringMuxPipeline {
    ring: IoUring,
    registered_buffers: Vec<Vec<u8>>,
    registered_fds: Vec<RawFd>,
    pending_ops: HashMap<u64, PendingOp>,
}

// Integration with asupersync via trait
pub trait IoReactor: Send + Sync {
    fn submit_read(&mut self, fd: RawFd, buf: &mut [u8]) -> Token;
    fn submit_write(&mut self, fd: RawFd, buf: &[u8]) -> Token;
    fn reap_completions(&mut self) -> Vec<(Token, io::Result<usize>)>;
}
```

## Tests
- **Criterion benchmarks**: 50 panes, measure syscalls/sec and throughput (before/after)
- Verify zero-copy: trace memory copies with strace (Linux)
- Stress test: 100 panes sustained for 1 hour, no FD leaks, no buffer corruption
- **Fallback test**: verify epoll path works when io_uring unavailable
- **macOS test**: verify kqueue fallback path works correctly
- Isomorphism proof: output is identical regardless of I/O backend

## Acceptance criteria
- io_uring pipeline works on Linux 5.6+
- epoll fallback for older Linux
- kqueue fallback for macOS
- Identical output regardless of backend
- Feature-gated: only compiled when requested
- 2-3x throughput improvement on Linux with io_uring","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:06:43.093457Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:34:30.174720Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-283h4.4","depends_on_id":"ft-283h4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-283h4.4","depends_on_id":"ft-brc7d","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-283h4.4","depends_on_id":"ft-e34d9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-15T21:29:20Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-283h4.5","title":"Reactive dataflow graph for declarative agent orchestration","description":"# Reactive Dataflow Graph for Declarative Agent Orchestration

## Why This Is Novel
wa-3dfxb.9 (Extension API) provides event hooks. wa-3dfxb.13 (Native event hooks) replaces Lua callbacks. wa-1l2o (Error cascade circuit breaker) is one specific policy. This provides the FRAMEWORK for composable, declarative, glitch-free orchestration that makes all of those more powerful.

## The Problem with Imperative Event Handlers
Current: "when event X, do Y." This doesn't compose. Real orchestration needs:
- "When pane A outputs ERROR AND pane B CPU > 90% AND cooldown elapsed → restart C"
- This requires manual state tracking, race condition handling, deduplication
- Imperative handlers become spaghetti at scale

## Reactive Dataflow Solution
Inspired by Jane Street's Incremental and Salsa (rust-analyzer):

```rust
// Declarative orchestration rules
let graph = DataflowGraph::new();

// Observe pane outputs
let pane_a_errors = graph.observe(pane_a, |output| output.contains("ERROR"));
let pane_b_cpu = graph.observe(pane_b, |metrics| metrics.cpu_percent);

// Combine with logic
let high_load = pane_b_cpu.map(|cpu| cpu > 90.0);
let cooldown = graph.timer(Duration::from_secs(300));

let should_restart = graph.combine3(
    pane_a_errors, high_load, cooldown,
    |error, load, cooled| *error && *load && *cooled,
);

// React
should_restart.on_change(|val| if *val { restart_pane(pane_c) });
```

## Key Properties
1. **Glitch-free**: Intermediate states NEVER trigger actions. If both pane_a_errors and high_load change simultaneously, the combined node is recomputed ONCE with both new values (topological sort propagation).
2. **Incremental**: Only recomputes affected subgraph when inputs change. 50-pane graph with 1 pane changing: recompute ~5 nodes, not all 200.
3. **Cycle-safe**: Static analysis at graph construction detects cycles and rejects them.
4. **Debuggable**: Entire graph serializable to JSON for visualization. Replay: feed recorded inputs, verify same outputs.
5. **Composable**: Graphs can be merged. Extension A's graph + Extension B's graph = combined graph.

## Architecture
```
Inputs (pane state, metrics, timers)
    │
    ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│      Reactive Dataflow Graph      │
│  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐      │
│  │Src A│→─│Map  │→─│Comb │→─┐   │
│  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘  │   │
│  ┌─────┐              ▲     ▼   │
│  │Src B│──────────────┘  ┌────┐ │
│  └─────┘                 │Sink│ │
│                          └────┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────┘
    │
    ▼
Actions (restart, notify, log, send input)
```

## Node Types
- **Source**: Observes external state (pane output, metrics, timers, config)
- **Map**: Transforms a single input (filter, threshold, parse)
- **Combine**: Merges multiple inputs with a function
- **Debounce**: Suppresses rapid changes (configurable window)
- **Sink**: Executes actions when input changes

## Integration with Extension System
WASM extensions (wa-3dfxb.4) can define dataflow nodes:
```rust
// In WASM extension
#[ft_dataflow_node]
fn classify_output(output: &str) -> OutputClass {
    if output.contains("rate limit") { OutputClass::RateLimit }
    else if output.contains("error") { OutputClass::Error }
    else { OutputClass::Normal }
}
```

- crates/wa-core/src/dataflow.rs (NEW — core graph engine)
- crates/wa-core/src/dataflow_nodes.rs (NEW — built-in node types)
- crates/wa/src/orchestration.rs (NEW — CLI for defining rules)

## Testing
- Unit: graph with known topology → verify propagation order
- Glitch-freedom: simultaneous input changes → verify single combined output
- Cycle detection: reject cyclic graphs at construction time
- Incremental: modify one input, count recomputed nodes (should be minimal)
- E2E: define a restart rule, trigger conditions, verify restart happens
- Benchmark: 200-node graph, 100 input changes/sec, <1ms total propagation time

## Depends on
- wa-3dfxb.13 (Native event hooks) — dataflow graph fires native events
- wa-3dfxb.9 (Extension API) — WASM extensions define dataflow nodes

 {"id":"ft-e34d9","title":"Epic: Replace tokio async runtime with asupersync","description":"# Epic: Replace tokio async runtime with asupersync

## Background
FrankenTerm currently uses tokio 1.49.0 (full features) as its async runtime, with ~1,090 async patterns across 61 files. The asupersync library (https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/asupersync) is a spec-first, cancel-correct, capability-secure Rust async runtime that replaces tokio entirely.

## Why asupersync?
1. **Structured concurrency** - Every spawned task belongs to a Scope; no orphan tasks possible
2. **Cancel-correctness** - Multi-phase cancellation protocol (Request→Drain→Finalize) vs tokio's silent drop
3. **Capability security** - All effects gated through Cx context token; no ambient authority
4. **Four-valued Outcome<T,E>** - Ok/Err/Cancelled/Panicked vs Result's Ok/Err
5. **Two-phase channel ops** - Reserve/Commit pattern eliminates "message lost on cancellation" bugs
6. **Deterministic testing** - LabRuntime with virtual time, seed-controlled scheduling, DPOR exploration
7. **Built-in networking** - TCP/UDP/TLS/HTTP/gRPC without tokio transitive deps

## Scope
Replace ALL tokio usage across the codebase:
- Core runtime and task spawning → asupersync Scope/Cx
- Sync primitives (Mutex, RwLock, Semaphore) → asupersync sync
- Channels (mpsc, watch, broadcast) → asupersync channels
- Network I/O (UnixStream, TcpListener) → asupersync net
- Time (sleep, timeout) → asupersync via Cx
- select! macro → asupersync combinators
- tokio-rustls → asupersync TLS
- reqwest → asupersync HTTP client
- Test infrastructure → LabRuntime

## Key Migration Patterns
| Tokio | Asupersync |
|-------|------------|
| #[tokio::main] | RuntimeBuilder::new().build()?.block_on(...) |
| tokio::spawn(fut) | scope.spawn(fut) inside cx.region() |
| tokio::select! | asupersync::combinator::select/race |
| tokio::time::sleep | cx.sleep(duration) |
| tokio::sync::mpsc | asupersync::channel::mpsc (reserve/commit) |
| tokio::sync::Mutex | asupersync::sync::Mutex (guard obligation) |
| Result<T,E> | Outcome<T,E> (four-valued) |
| Drop cancellation | cx.checkpoint() explicit cancellation points |

## Critical Consideration
All async functions gain a `cx: &mut Cx` parameter. This is the most pervasive change - it threads through the entire call graph. Plan function signature changes carefully to minimize churn.

 {"id":"ft-e6pq","title":"Layout restoration engine — recreate window/tab/split topology from snapshot","description":"## Goal
Implement the layout restoration engine that recreates the exact window/tab/pane split topology from a MuxSnapshot, positioning panes in the same arrangement they had before the mux server restart.

## Background & Motivation
After a mux server restart, all windows, tabs, and split panes are gone. The restoration engine must recreate the exact same layout using WezTerm CLI commands (wezterm cli spawn, wezterm cli split-pane). This is the visual foundation — users should see the same window arrangement they had before.

WezTerm's resurrect.wezterm plugin does approximate layout restoration via Lua but has limitations: it can't restore exact split ratios, and it struggles with complex nested splits. Our approach uses the vendored mux client and exact pane geometry data captured in the MuxSnapshot.

## Technical Design

### Struct Definition
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_layout.rs
pub struct LayoutRestorer {
    mux_client: Arc<DirectMuxClient>,
    config: RestoreConfig,
}

pub struct RestoreConfig {
    pub restore_working_dirs: bool,  // default: true
    pub restore_split_ratios: bool,  // default: true
    pub restore_window_positions: bool, // default: false (OS may differ)
}

// Maps old pane IDs to new pane IDs after restoration
pub type PaneIdMap = HashMap<PaneId, PaneId>;
```

### Core Algorithm
1. For each window in the snapshot:
   a. Create a new window (wezterm cli spawn --new-window)
   b. Record the new window_id
2. For each tab in the window:
   a. If not the first tab, create new tab (wezterm cli spawn-tab)
   b. Record the new tab_id
3. For the pane tree within each tab:
   a. Use recursive descent on the PaneNode tree
   b. For Split nodes: create split-pane with correct direction (horizontal/vertical)
   c. For Leaf nodes: record the pane_id mapping (old -> new)
   d. Adjust split ratios using resize commands

### Recursive Split Restoration
```rust
async fn restore_pane_tree(
    &self,
    node: &PaneNode,
    parent_pane_id: PaneId,
    id_map: &mut PaneIdMap,
) -> Result<PaneId> {
    match node {
        PaneNode::Leaf { pane_id, cwd, .. } => {
            id_map.insert(*pane_id, parent_pane_id);
            if self.config.restore_working_dirs {
                // Set working directory via shell command
                self.set_cwd(parent_pane_id, cwd).await?;
            }
            Ok(parent_pane_id)
        }
        PaneNode::Split { direction, children, ratio, .. } => {
            // First child uses the existing pane
            let first = self.restore_pane_tree(&children[0], parent_pane_id, id_map).await?;
            // Second child gets a new split pane
            let new_pane = self.create_split(parent_pane_id, *direction).await?;
            let second = self.restore_pane_tree(&children[1], new_pane, id_map).await?;
            // Adjust split ratio
            if self.config.restore_split_ratios {
                self.adjust_ratio(first, second, *ratio).await?;
            }
            Ok(first)
        }
    }
}
```

## Integration Points
- **MuxSnapshot schema** (bd-cuz): Reads PaneNode tree structure
- **DirectMuxClient**: Uses spawn, split-pane, and resize PDUs
- **WeztermClient** (wezterm.rs): Fallback to CLI for operations not in mux protocol
- **PaneIdMap**: Returned to caller for use by scrollback injection and process re-launch

## Key Files to Create/Modify
- CREATE: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_layout.rs
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/mod.rs (add module)
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs (may need spawn/split PDU support)

## Edge Cases
- Window manager may not honor exact window positions (especially across restarts)
- Split ratios may need multiple resize iterations to converge
- If mux server has existing panes, restoration should create in addition (not replace)
- Tab titles should be restored from snapshot metadata

## Dependencies
- bd-cuz: MuxSnapshot schema (PaneNode tree definition)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Single window with multiple tabs restores correctly
- Horizontal and vertical splits restore with correct topology
- Nested splits (3+ levels deep) restore correctly
- Split ratios are approximately correct (within 5% of original)
- Working directories are set correctly for each pane
- PaneIdMap correctly maps all old pane IDs to new ones
- Handles graceful errors (e.g., if a split fails, continues with remaining)

## Estimated Effort
2-3 hours implementation, 1 hour testing

## Test Requirements

### Property-Based Testing (proptest)
Add proptest cases in `crates/wa-core/tests/proptest_layout_restore.rs`:
- **Layout roundtrip invariant**: For any arbitrary layout tree, `save(restore(save(layout))) == save(layout)` — saving a restored layout must produce an identical snapshot to the original. This is the fundamental correctness property. Strategy: `arb_layout_tree()` generates random PaneNode trees with 1-50 panes, random split directions, and random ratios in [0.1, 0.9].
- **PaneIdMap completeness**: For any layout tree, after restoration the PaneIdMap must contain exactly one entry for every leaf pane in the original tree (no missing mappings, no extra mappings).
- **Topology preservation**: For any layout tree, the structural shape (split directions, nesting depth, child ordering) of the restored layout must exactly match the original, regardless of specific pane IDs.
- **Split ratio fidelity**: For any layout tree with split ratios, restored ratios must be within 5% of originals. Strategy: `arb_ratio()` generates f64 in [0.1, 0.9].

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add benchmarks in `crates/wa-core/benches/layout_restore.rs`:
- `bench_restore_50_pane_layout`: Restore a 50-pane complex layout (10 tabs, mixed splits), target <500ms
- `bench_restore_single_tab_deep_splits`: Restore a single tab with 10-level deep nested splits
- `bench_pane_id_map_construction`: PaneIdMap construction for 200 panes, target <1ms

- **wa-2dd4s.2** (Floating panes): Floating (overlay) panes are not part of the split tree — they exist as separate overlay layers.
